professor.name: Dr. Moreno
professor.text: We've been examining how cities can cool themselves as summers grow hotter this semester. One proposal that keeps coming up is requiring green roofs — rooftops planted with vegetation — on all new commercial buildings. Some planners argue this cuts energy use and manages stormwater cheaply, while others counter that the upkeep costs and structural demands make it an unfair burden on developers. Should cities mandate green roofs on new construction? Why or why not?

students.name: Lena
students.text: Mandating green roofs makes sense because individual developers rarely account for the collective benefits their buildings could provide. Left to the market, almost no one installs them, so a citywide requirement is the only realistic way to reach the scale needed to meaningfully reduce urban heat.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I'm skeptical that a blanket rule is the right tool here. Green roofs demand reinforced structures and constant maintenance, and forcing them onto every project could simply raise housing costs. Targeted incentives would achieve comparable environmental gains without penalizing smaller developers.

professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: A number of European firms have recently started guaranteeing employees a "right to disconnect" — no work emails or messages after hours, sometimes enforced by formal policy. Supporters say this protects wellbeing and even improves productivity, while critics argue it strips away flexibility that many workers value and is difficult to enforce across time zones. Should companies formally limit after-hours communication with employees? What's your view?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: Companies should absolutely set clear boundaries. Without an explicit policy, employees feel pressured to answer messages late at night just to appear committed, and that expectation gradually erodes any real separation between work and personal life. A formal rule usefully shifts the norm for everyone at once.

students.name: Ross
students.text: I understand the concern, but a rigid ban strikes me as impractical. Plenty of people genuinely prefer flexible schedules, answering a quick message in the evening so they can leave early another day. I'd rather companies respect individual preferences than impose one uniform standard on everyone.

professor.name: Dr. Halstead
professor.text: More and more people now fill every idle moment — waiting in line, riding the bus — by reaching for a phone. Some psychologists warn that this constant stimulation crowds out the mental downtime our brains need to consolidate ideas, while others say boredom is merely unpleasant and that staying engaged keeps us sharp. Do you think people should deliberately allow themselves more unstructured, boredom-prone time? Why?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think intentionally embracing boredom is genuinely valuable. When the mind isn't being fed constant input, it begins to wander, and that wandering is often where unexpected connections and creative solutions emerge. Filling every gap with a screen means we never give those quieter mental processes a chance.

students.name: Paul
students.text: The idea sounds appealing, but I'm not convinced boredom itself deserves the credit. Meaningful reflection can happen just as easily while listening to a podcast or reading something absorbing. What matters is the quality of what occupies our attention, not whether we force ourselves to sit idle.

professor.name: Dr. Vance
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about lowering the voting age to sixteen. A handful of countries and cities already permit it in local elections. Advocates argue that teenagers are directly affected by policies on education and climate and are mature enough to weigh in, while skeptics question whether most sixteen-year-olds have the experience to cast informed votes. Should the voting age be lowered to sixteen?

students.name: Carlos
students.text: Lowering the voting age would strengthen democracy. Sixteen-year-olds can work and pay taxes, yet have no formal say in the policies shaping their futures. Bringing them into the process earlier would likely build lifelong voting habits and push politicians to take young people's concerns seriously.

students.name: Zoe
students.text: I'm hesitant about this. Sound political judgment tends to develop alongside real-world experience — managing finances, holding a steady job — that most sixteen-year-olds simply haven't accumulated yet. Extending the vote to them risks amplifying opinions still heavily shaped by parents or teachers.
